evidence: The paper uses scikit-learn random forest with default impurity-based importance (not specified but standard Gini/MDI for classification). It ranks features explicitly: "5-aminovaleric acid as the most important compound" and "5-(Carbamoylamino)pentanoic acid, which had the second highest contribution in the random forest model." The authors interpret these rankings biologically: "The substance that contributed the most to the classification, 5-aminovaleric acid, is known to act as a weak GABA receptor agonist...may be associated with increased gene expression of Chrna4." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation was performed to corroborate the feature rankings.
